intro: Allium sativum cream, also known as garlic cream, is a topical treatment that utilizes the antibacterial and antifungal properties of garlic to promote wound healing and prevent infection. This cream is often applied to minor cuts and scrapes to reduce inflammation and promote tissue repair. However, it's essential to note that allium sativum cream should not be used on open wounds, deep cuts, or sensitive areas without consulting a medical professional first.
side_effects: Allium sativum cream, derived from garlic, is generally considered safe when applied topically, but excessive use or sensitivity can lead to skin irritation. Allium sativum cream can have side effects such as skin redness, itching, and burning sensations in rare cases. Additionally, individuals with allergies or sensitivities to garlic or other members of the Allium family may experience more severe reactions.
uses: Allium sativum cream is used to treat various skin conditions, including acne, eczema, and dermatitis, due to its anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties. It may also help to soothe and calm irritated skin, reducing redness and promoting wound healing. Additionally, allium sativum cream has been traditionally used to treat minor cuts and scrapes, as well as to reduce the appearance of scars.

recipe: To make allium sativum cream for medicinal use, combine 1 part of Allium sativum (also known as garlic) oil with 9 parts of a neutral oil carrier such as coconut or olive oil, and mix well with a thickening agent like beeswax and shea butter. The resulting mixture should be heated to a temperature of around 120°F to 140°F (49°C to 60°C) until the beeswax is fully incorporated, then allowed to cool and thicken. Once set, the cream can be stored in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 6 months.
